summary: Patrick McKenzie shares his complete salary and consulting rate history to support the #talkpay movement, arguing that information asymmetry in compensation negotiations systematically disadvantages employees. He traces his journey from $10/hr order entry through Japanese salaryman wages to consulting rates of $30k-$50k per week, demonstrating that the difference between low and high compensation is rarely about skill but about positioning, packaging, and market knowledge. He argues that well-compensated professionals have a moral obligation to share compensation data to help those earlier in their careers. The post also covers equity valuation, business sales, and negotiation tactics.
key_points: Employers have near-perfect salary information while candidates often have none, creating a structural negotiating disadvantage equivalent to trading on a stock exchange where only the counterparty can see the ticker

The difference between $100/hr and $30k/week consulting was not about skill improvement but about targeting higher-value engagements, better packaging, and overcoming impostor syndrome about charging premium rates
Charging premium rates creates a virtuous cycle: you can be picky about engagements, deliver better results, build stronger case studies, and negotiate from a position of strength
Never disclose a previous salary because it pegs your new compensation to that number plus a small increment rather than your actual value to the new employer
Well-compensated professionals should share salary data as breadcrumbs for those still leveling up, since nobody is born with reliable market data
money_quotes: Compensation negotiations are presently like a stock exchange where only your counterparty can see the ticker and order book. You'd never send an order to that exchange — it would be an invitation to be fleeced.
I was nearly exactly the same level of capabilities at $30k a year as I was at $30k a week, I just got better at organizing the business to expose me to opportunities to capture the better rate.

There is virtually no difference in the mechanics of work done between $100 an hour, $200 an hour, and $30k a week — all of the leveling up there is in sophistication on who you go after, what engagements you propose and deliver, and how you package things for clients.
Disclosing a previous salary is almost always against your interests because it pegs your new salary to that plus 5% rather than your value to the new firm minus a discount.
Pick a number, any number: somebody does a lot better than that. For most numbers you'd naively think of, its an awful lot of somebodies. $100k? Not the top. $250k? Not the top. $500k? … Not the top.
If a client says you're out of the budget, start talking about the scope of the engagement to find something they can afford rather than slipping your rate.
The main thing holding my rate down for the early years was personal discomfort with being 'worth' the types of rates which I desired to charge.
Don't be hesitant to be compensated equitably for the value you are creating.
key_insight: The gap between low and high compensation is primarily about information asymmetry and self-positioning, not skill — the same person can earn 50x more by targeting higher-value work and overcoming the discomfort of charging what they're worth.
